system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task H: Sentencing reform for non-violent drug offenses

## Context

You are advising the legislature of a mid-sized state (population ~6M, incarceration rate ~750/100K - above national median) on reforming its sentencing framework for non-violent drug possession and low-level distribution offenses. Context: prison population has grown 38% over 10 years despite stable underlying drug-use rates; ~31% of state prison population is held on drug-related charges; recidivism within 3 years of release is 47%; budgetary pressure on corrections is acute (~$1.8B/year). The reform must be politically feasible (signed by a moderate governor), legally defensible, and trackable for outcomes.

Five candidate reform packages have been drafted. **Choose one as the proposed framework.** This will define the bill that goes to floor vote.

## Options

**Option A: Mandatory minimum elimination + sentencing guidelines**
Remove mandatory minimums for non-violent drug offenses entirely. Replace with non-binding judge-discretion guidelines. Predicate offenses graded by quantity and prior record. Outcomes: judges regain discretion; sentencing disparities possibly increase or decrease depending on judges; potential rapid decarceration. Risks: inconsistency between counties; possible political backlash if any high-profile lenient sentence emerges; doesn't address root causes.

**Option B: Treatment-court diversion (mandated)**
First and second non-violent drug offenders are mandatorily diverted to drug treatment court instead of incarceration. Completion of program clears record; failure routes back to prison. Outcomes: evidence-based; aligned with public health framing; reduces recidivism for completers (literature: -25 to -45%). Risks: high treatment capacity required (currently insufficient); treatment-court compliance can be coercive; selection effects (only motivated complete); doesn't reach distribution-level offenses.

**Option C: Categorical decriminalization (possession only)**
Possession of small amounts of any controlled substance becomes a civil infraction (fine, no incarceration, no criminal record). Distribution and trafficking remain criminal. Outcomes: dramatic and fast decarceration; clear public-health framing; precedent from Portugal/Oregon (mixed results); removes barriers to housing/employment for users. Risks: federal-state conflict (federal drug law unchanged); political fragility; doesn't address overdose mortality directly; possible signal-of-permissiveness concerns.

**Option D: Retroactive resentencing of currently-incarcerated**
Existing inmates serving sentences for offenses below the proposed thresholds are automatically resentenced under new guidelines, with parole boards reviewing for early release. Future sentencing rules from Option A or B layer on top. Outcomes: immediate impact on current prison population; aligned with retroactive-equity reasoning; well-documented effect in California Prop 47. Risks: parole-board capacity bottleneck; victims' rights groups oppose; some released inmates re-offend, creating political crisis.

**Option E: Comprehensive harm-reduction package**
Combines: limited decriminalization (Option C), expanded treatment access via tax-funded clinics, supervised consumption sites, naloxone distribution, expanded fentanyl test strips, prescription heroin pilot for chronic users. No retroactive resentencing in initial bill. Outcomes: most ambitious; explicit public-health framing; addresses overdose deaths directly; expensive (~$200-400M/year additional). Risks: politically toughest; conservative jurisdictions and police unions oppose; may need federal waivers; long timeline to outcomes (3-5 years).

## What to provide

Choose one as the proposed framework. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this option, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific outcome 3 years from now would make you regret this choice and what you would argue at that point.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
Choose **Option B**. Mandatory diversion to treatment court for first- and second-time non-violent drug offenders offers the optimal alignment of evidence-based efficacy, immediate fiscal relief, and political durability for a moderate administration confronting a 47 percent recidivism rate and a corrections budget consuming $1.8 billion annually. Rather than simply opening prison doors through decriminalization or early release, this framework converts incarceration spending into supervised recovery, attacking the root behavioral driver that fills beds. By tethering record clearance to program completion, the law preserves accountability while removing the lifelong barriers to employment and housing that perpetuate the revolving door. It also gives the governor a defensible narrative: this is not leniency, but leverage.

The most compelling alternative is Option A’s elimination of mandatory minimums paired with judicial discretion. While discretion can reduce excessive sentences, it is a blunt instrument without the therapeutic infrastructure necessary to lower recidivism; it merely shifts variance from the statute to the courtroom, exposing the entire reform to fracture the moment one judge hands down a headline-grabbing lenient sentence. Option B, by contrast, channels every eligible offender into a standardized, measurable intervention whose outcomes—completion rates, time-to-placement, and three-year recidivism—can be tracked with precision.

Three years from now, I would regret this choice if waiting lists for treatment placement exceed six months and completion rates stagnate below 35 percent, proving that the mandate outran the state’s capacity to deliver care. At that point, I would argue that the framework itself is not the error; the failure is an appropriations gap. The legislature must capture the savings from averted prison beds and redirect them immediately into medication-assisted treatment, residential slots, and court staffing, preserving the architecture while filling its foundation.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to choose one of five sentencing reform options for a mid-sized state and provide a 200-400 word recommendation. I need to:

1. Choose one option as the proposed framework
2. Provide reasoning in 200-400 words
3. Advocate for the position (not just enumerate)
4. Explain why this option over the most compelling alternative
5. Identify what specific outcome 3 years from now would make me regret this choice
6. Explain what I would argue at that point

Let me analyze the options:

Option A: Mandatory minimum elimination + sentencing guidelines. Pros: judicial discretion, rapid decarceration. Cons: inconsistency, political backlash risk, doesn't address root causes.

Option B: Treatment-court diversion. Pros: evidence-based, reduces recidivism for completers, public health framing. Cons: requires high treatment capacity (currently insufficient), coercive, selection effects, doesn't cover distribution.

Option C: Categorical decriminalization (possession only). Pros: fast decarceration, removes barriers. Cons: federal-state conflict, political fragility, doesn't address overdose mortality.

Option D: Retroactive resentencing. Pros: immediate impact, retroactive equity. Cons: parole board bottleneck, victims' rights opposition, re-offending risk creates political crisis.

Option E: Comprehensive harm-reduction. Pros: most ambitious, addresses overdose deaths. Cons: politically toughest, expensive, long timeline.

Given the constraints:
- Population ~6M, incarceration rate high
- 31% of prison population drug-related
- Budgetary pressure acute ($1.8B/year)
- Must be politically feasible (moderate governor)
- Legally defensible
- Trackable outcomes

Political feasibility is a key constraint. Option E is likely too politically tough. Option C might be too politically fragile. Option D has high political risk with victims' rights groups and re-offending. Option A doesn't address root causes and risks backlash.

Option B seems most balanced: evidence-based, politically viable (moderate governors can support treatment courts), legally defensible, trackable outcomes. However, it doesn't reach distribution-level offenses and requires treatment capacity.

Wait, the prompt says "Choose one as the proposed framework" and the user wants advocacy. But I need to be careful - is there a best answer? Let me think about which is actually most defensible.

Actually, looking at the political feasibility constraint (moderate governor), Option B is very strong. Drug treatment courts have bipartisan support. But the capacity issue is real.

Option A is also moderate-governor feasible but risks county inconsistency and backlash.

What about a hybrid? No, I must choose one of the five.

Let me reconsider Option B vs Option A. Option B is the most compelling alternative to whichever I choose.

Actually, I think Option B might be the best choice because:
1. Politically feasible - moderate governors love treatment courts (tough but compassionate)
2. Evidence-based recidivism reduction
3. Addresses the budget issue by reducing incarceration (though requires treatment investment)
4. Legally defensible - drug courts are well-established
5. Trackable - completion rates, recidivism, etc.

But the prompt asks me to address "why not the most compelling alternative." If I choose B, the most compelling alternative might be A (rapid decarceration) or E (comprehensive approach). If I choose A, the alternative might be B.

Wait, I should think about what makes the most strategic sense. The state has an incarceration rate of 750/100K (above national median), 31% drug-related, budget pressure. The governor is moderate.

Option B's mandatory diversion for first and second offenders would directly address the prison population growth and recidivism, but the capacity issue is a major risk.

Option A gives discretion back to judges. It's cleaner legally, allows case-by-case judgment, but risks the "Willie Horton" problem if someone gets lenient sentence and reoffends.

Let me think... For a moderate governor, Option B is probably safer politically because it doesn't "let people off" - it mandates treatment. It's active and supervised. Option C is too permissive for a moderate. Option E too expensive and radical. Option D too risky with current inmates.

I think I'll recommend Option B, but I need to check if the word count works.

Wait, actually, I could make a strong case for Option A. Mandatory minimums are a key driver of prison growth. Removing them with guidelines allows tailored justice while maintaining structure. It's legally clean and trackable. But the prompt says it "doesn't address root causes."

Alternatively, what if I recommend Option B but acknowledge the capacity issue?

Let me draft for Option B:

"Choose Option B: Treatment-court diversion. This framework offers the optimal balance of evidence-based efficacy, fiscal prudence, and political durability for a moderate administration. Unlike decriminalization, it avoids the federalism quagmire and 'signal-of-permissiveness' attacks that would fracture the coalition needed to pass the bill. Unlike retroactive resentencing, it sidesteps the parole-board bottleneck and volatile optics of early release. And unlike mere guideline reform, it actually treats addiction rather than outsourcing discretion to individual judges.

The most compelling alternative is Option A, which promises faster decarceration. But sentencing discretion without treatment infrastructure merely swaps prison cells for revolving-door courts; it neither lowers recidivism nor builds the public-health capacity this population needs. Option B directly attacks the 47% recidivism rate by addressing substance-use disorders as the driver of repeat offending, while the 'mandatory' nature of diversion insulates the governor from charges of being 'soft on crime.'

Three years from now, I would regret this choice if program-completion rates fall below 35% and waiting lists for treatment exceed six months, turning diversion into a delaying tactic rather than a therapeutic intervention. At that point, I would argue that the legislature failed to appropriate sufficient front-end capacity funding, not that the diversion model itself is flawed; the fix is to reinvest the savings from averted prison beds into residential and medication-assisted treatment slots, preserving the framework while fixing the resource mismatch."

Word count check: That's about 215 words. Good.

Actually, let me reconsider. Is Option B really the best? The user says "treatment-court compliance can be coercive; selection effects (only motivated complete); doesn't reach distribution-level offenses." These are valid concerns. But for a moderate state, it's the most passable.

Alternatively, I could recommend Option A and argue against B:

"Option A removes the structural engine of prison growth—mandatory minimums—while preserving judicial accountability through transparent guidelines. Unlike Option B, it does not require massive immediate treatment capacity that the state lacks, nor does it coerce defendants into courts they may fail. The most compelling alternative, B, promises recidivism reduction but only for the minority who complete programs; without existing capacity, mandatory diversion becomes a failed mandate. Option A achieves decarceration without manufacturing new system bottlenecks..."

Hmm. But Option A "doesn't address root causes" and risks inconsistency.

I think Option B is stronger because the prompt emphasizes recidivism (47%) and budget pressure. Treatment courts reduce recidivism. And the moderate governor constraint makes B attractive.

Wait, I need to ensure I don't enumerate. The prompt says "Advocate; do not enumerate." So I shouldn't list "Option A is bad because... Option C is bad because..." I should instead focus on advocating for my chosen option and only contrast with the most compelling alternative.

Let me refine the Option B draft to be more flowing and less enumerative.

Draft 2:

Choose Option B. Mandatory diversion to treatment court for first- and second-time non-violent drug offenders is the only framework that converts the state’s corrections spending into a public-health investment without triggering the political, legal, and logistical landmines that doom broader reforms. It gives the moderate governor a clear narrative: tough accountability through supervised treatment, not blanket decriminalization or arbitrary early release. By tying record clearance to completion, the law creates a powerful incentive structure that keeps participants engaged while directly attacking the 47% recidivism rate that is driving prison growth and budget hemorrhage.

The most compelling alternative is Option A’s sentencing-guideline approach, which offers judicial discretion and faster decarceration. Yet discretion without infrastructure is an empty promise; it neither treats addiction nor guarantees consistent outcomes, and a single high-profile lenient sentence could unravel the entire reform. Option B, by contrast, is legally battle-tested, trackable through completion metrics, and shielded from the county-level inconsistency that guidelines invite.

In three years, I would regret this choice if waiting lists for treatment exceed six months and completion rates stagnate below one-third, proving the state funded the mandate without building capacity. Should that occur, the argument must not be to abandon diversion, but to double down: redirect the savings from averted prison beds into medication-assisted treatment and residential slots, because the framework is sound even when the appropriation is not.

Word count: ~215 words. Good.

Let me double-check requirements:
- 200-400 words ✓
- Choose one as proposed framework ✓
- Advocate; do not enumerate ✓ (I mention Option A only as the alternative to reject, not enumerate all)
- Why this option ✓
- Why not the most compelling alternative ✓
- What specific outcome 3 years from now would make me regret this choice ✓
- What I would argue at that point ✓
- Specific outcome: waiting lists >6 months, completion rates <1/3 ✓

Actually, looking at the prompt again: "Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this option, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific outcome 3 years from now would make you regret this choice and what you would argue at that point."

Wait, the user says "Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words." So the whole response should be 200-400 words.

My draft is about 215 words. That's good. Let me make sure it's polished and flows well.

One concern: Option B "doesn't reach distribution-level offenses." But the prompt says the reform is for "non-violent drug possession and low-level distribution offenses." Wait, Option B says "First and second non-violent drug offenders... doesn't reach distribution-level offenses." Hmm, actually the prompt for Option B says "doesn't reach distribution-level offenses" but the context includes "low-level distribution." However, Option B says "First and second non-violent drug offenders are mandatorily diverted" - it doesn't explicitly exclude low-level distribution, but the risk note says it doesn't reach distribution-level. This is ambiguous. But the prompt says the legislature is reforming "sentencing framework for non-violent drug possession and low-level distribution offenses." So Option B might not cover low-level distribution. That's a weakness.

Option A covers both possession and distribution (predicate offenses graded by quantity). Option C is possession only. Option D is below proposed thresholds. Option E is limited decriminalization (possession only? It says "limited decriminalization (Option C)" so possession only.

Actually, the context says reforming "sentencing framework for non-violent drug possession and low-level distribution offenses." Option B's risk says "doesn't reach distribution-level offenses." So if the legislature wants to address both, Option B is incomplete.

Option A explicitly mentions "Predicate offenses graded by quantity" which covers distribution.

Option D mentions "offenses below the proposed thresholds" - could cover distribution.

But wait, the prompt says "Five candidate reform packages have been drafted. Choose one as the proposed framework." So each option is a complete package. Option B only covering possession might be a significant limitation.

However, the prompt says Option B is for "First and second non-violent drug offenders" - "drug offenders" could include low-level distribution. The risk note "doesn't reach distribution-level offenses" might mean it doesn't address higher-level distribution, not necessarily low-level. Or it could mean the diversion program itself doesn't cover distribution charges. The text is slightly ambiguous.

Given the instruction to choose one and advocate, I can interpret this charitably or choose a different option.

Let me consider Option A again. It covers both possession and distribution by quantity. It removes mandatory minimums entirely. It's politically feasible for a moderate governor? Maybe, if framed as "judicial discretion with accountability through guidelines." But the risk is high-profile lenient sentences.

What if I choose Option B but acknowledge it applies to possession and low-level distribution as appropriate for diversion courts? The risk note might be overstated.

Alternatively, what about Option D + A? The prompt says "Future sentencing rules from Option A or B layer on top." So Option D is retroactive + future rules. But the prompt says Option D has risks of parole-board bottleneck and victims' rights opposition.

I think Option B remains the strongest politically. But let me consider if there's a way to advocate for Option A more effectively.

Option A argument:
"Eliminating mandatory minimums replaces a one-size-fails-all machinery that has driven a 38% prison population surge with transparent, evidence-based guidelines that respect judicial expertise while maintaining accountability. For a moderate governor, this is the fiscally responsible choice: it immediately relieves budgetary pressure by halting the pipeline of unnecessarily long sentences without requiring the massive new spending that treatment infrastructure or supervised consumption sites demand. The most compelling alternative is Option B, but mandatory diversion is only as strong as the treatment beds behind it, and building that capacity in a state with insufficient resources risks turning a therapeutic mandate into a procedural backlog that leaves offenders in pre-trial limbo. Option A avoids that trap; it reforms the law itself rather than betting on a service expansion that the legislature may underfund. Three years from now, I would regret this choice if sentencing disparities between counties widen by more than 20% and violent crime is wrongly attributed to lenient drug sentences, sparking a repeal movement. At that point, I would argue that the guidelines need tightening and that judicial training, not a return to mandatory minimums, is the appropriate remedy—because the flaw would lie in implementation variance, not in the principle that punishment should fit the individual rather than the statute."

This is also strong. It addresses the fiscal pressure directly ($1.8B/year). It avoids the capacity bottleneck. It handles both possession and low-level distribution.

But the prompt says Option A "doesn't address root causes" and "risks: inconsistency between counties; possible political backlash if any high-profile lenient sentence emerges." These are significant.

Which option would a moderate governor actually sign?
- Option B: Moderate governors frequently support drug courts. It's the safest "tough on crime, smart on justice" option.
- Option A: Moderate governors might sign this, but it's more vulnerable to "soft on crime" attacks.

But the parole/prosecutorial discretion issue... Hmm.

Let me think about the trackability requirement. Option B is very trackable (completion rates, recidivism rates). Option A is trackable (sentencing ranges, disparity metrics). Option C is trackable (civil infractions issued, overdoses). Option D is trackable (releases, re-offense). Option E is trackable (overdose deaths, clinic usage).

The user asks for the recommendation to be 200-400 words. My drafts are around 200-220. I should expand slightly to ensure I'm in the middle of the range, maybe 250-300 words, to be safe and more thorough.

Let me go with Option B, but make it robust.

Revised Option B draft:

Choose Option B. Mandatory diversion to treatment court for first- and second-time non-violent drug offenders offers the single best alignment of evidence-based outcomes, fiscal relief, and political viability for a moderate administration confronting a 47% recidivism rate and a corrections budget consuming $1.8 billion annually. Rather than simply opening prison doors through decriminalization or retroactive release, this framework converts incarceration spending into supervised recovery, attacking the root behavioral driver that fills beds. By tethering record clearance to program completion, the law preserves accountability while removing the lifelong barriers to employment and housing that perpetuate the revolving door. It also gives the governor a defensible narrative: this is not leniency, but leverage.

The most compelling alternative is Option A’s elimination of mandatory minimums paired with judicial discretion. While discretion can reduce excessive sentences, it is a blunt instrument without the therapeutic infrastructure necessary to lower recidivism; it merely shifts variance from the statute to the courtroom, exposing the reform to fracture the moment one judge hands down a headline-granting lenient sentence. Option B, by contrast, channels every eligible offender into a standardized, measurable intervention whose outcomes—completion rates, time-to-placement, and three-year recidivism—can be tracked precisely.

Three years from now, I would regret this choice if waiting lists for treatment placement exceed six months and completion rates stagnate below thirty-five percent, proving that the mandate outran the state’s capacity to deliver care. At that point, I would argue that the framework itself is not the error; the failure is an appropriations gap. The legislature must capture the savings from averted prison beds and redirect them immediately into medication-assisted treatment, residential slots, and court staffing, preserving the architecture while filling its foundation.

Word count check: ~285 words. That's solid.

Let me verify against constraints:
- 200-400 words? Yes (~285).
- Advocate; do not enumerate? Yes, I only mention Option A as the alternative, no listing of all options.
- Why this option? Yes (evidence-based, fiscal, political, addresses root driver).
- Why not the most compelling alternative? Yes (Option A is blunt, shifts variance, exposes to fracture).
- What specific outcome 3 years from now would make me regret? Yes (waiting lists >6 months, completion <35%).
- What would I argue at that point? Yes (framework is sound, appropriations gap, redirect savings into MAT/residential/staffing).

One small concern: Option B's risk says "high treatment capacity required (currently insufficient)." My draft addresses this in the regret/argument section by saying if capacity fails, fund it with prison savings. This is a good way to handle it.

Another concern: Option B "doesn't reach distribution-level offenses." The context mentions "low-level distribution." If I need to address this implicitly, I can note "first- and second-time non-violent drug offenders" which could include low-level distribution. The risk note might mean higher-level distribution. I'll proceed.

The tone should be analytical but advocating. My draft does that.

I think this is ready.

"Eliminating mandatory minimums replaces a one-size-fails-all machinery that has driven a 38% prison population surge with transparent, evidence-based guidelines that respect judicial expertise while maintaining accountability. For a moderate governor, this is the fiscally responsible choice: it immediately relieves budgetary pressure by halting the pipeline of unnecessarily long sentences without requiring the massive new spending that treatment infrastructure or supervised consumption sites demand. The most compelling alternative is Option B, but mandatory diversion is only as strong as the treatment beds behind it, and building that capacity in a state with insufficient resources risks turning a therapeutic mandate into a procedural backlog that leaves offenders in pre-trial limbo. Option A avoids that trap; it reforms the law itself rather than betting on a service expansion that the legislature may underfund. Three years from now, I would regret this choice if sentencing disparities between counties widen by more than 20% and violent crime is wrongly attributed to lenient drug sentences, sparking a repeal movement. Choose Option B. Mandatory diversion to treatment court for first- and second-time non-violent drug offenders offers the single best alignment of evidence-based outcomes, fiscal relief, and political viability for a moderate administration confronting a 47% recidivism rate and a corrections budget consuming $1.8 billion annually. Rather than simply opening prison doors through decriminalization or retroactive release, this framework converts incarceration spending into supervised recovery, attacking the root behavioral driver that fills beds. By tethering record clearance to program completion, the law preserves accountability while removing the lifelong barriers to employment and housing that perpetuate the revolving door. It also gives the governor a defensible narrative: this is not leniency, but leverage.

The most compelling alternative is Option A’s elimination of mandatory minimums paired with judicial discretion. While discretion can reduce excessive sentences, it is a blunt instrument without the therapeutic infrastructure necessary to lower recidivism; it merely shifts variance from the statute to the courtroom, exposing the reform to fracture the moment one judge hands down a headline-granting lenient sentence. Option B, by contrast, channels every eligible offender into a standardized, measurable intervention whose outcomes—completion rates, time-to-placement, and three-year recidivism—can be tracked precisely.

Three years from now, I would regret this choice if waiting lists for treatment placement exceed six months and completion rates stagnate below thirty-five percent, proving that the mandate outran the state’s capacity to deliver care. At that point, I would argue that the framework itself is not the error; the failure is an appropriations gap. The legislature must capture the savings from averted prison beds and redirect them immediately into medication-assisted treatment, residential slots, and court staffing, preserving the architecture while filling its foundation.

messages.content: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.

# Task H: Sentencing reform for non-violent drug offenses

## Context

You are advising the legislature of a mid-sized state (population ~6M, incarceration rate ~750/100K - above national median) on reforming its sentencing framework for non-violent drug possession and low-level distribution offenses. Context: prison population has grown 38% over 10 years despite stable underlying drug-use rates; ~31% of state prison population is held on drug-related charges; recidivism within 3 years of release is 47%; budgetary pressure on corrections is acute (~$1.8B/year). The reform must be politically feasible (signed by a moderate governor), legally defensible, and trackable for outcomes.

Five candidate reform packages have been drafted. **Choose one as the proposed framework.** This will define the bill that goes to floor vote.

## Options

**Option A: Mandatory minimum elimination + sentencing guidelines**
Remove mandatory minimums for non-violent drug offenses entirely. Replace with non-binding judge-discretion guidelines. Predicate offenses graded by quantity and prior record. Outcomes: judges regain discretion; sentencing disparities possibly increase or decrease depending on judges; potential rapid decarceration. Risks: inconsistency between counties; possible political backlash if any high-profile lenient sentence emerges; doesn't address root causes.

**Option B: Treatment-court diversion (mandated)**
First and second non-violent drug offenders are mandatorily diverted to drug treatment court instead of incarceration. Completion of program clears record; failure routes back to prison. Outcomes: evidence-based; aligned with public health framing; reduces recidivism for completers (literature: -25 to -45%). Risks: high treatment capacity required (currently insufficient); treatment-court compliance can be coercive; selection effects (only motivated complete); doesn't reach distribution-level offenses.

**Option C: Categorical decriminalization (possession only)**
Possession of small amounts of any controlled substance becomes a civil infraction (fine, no incarceration, no criminal record). Distribution and trafficking remain criminal. Outcomes: dramatic and fast decarceration; clear public-health framing; precedent from Portugal/Oregon (mixed results); removes barriers to housing/employment for users. Risks: federal-state conflict (federal drug law unchanged); political fragility; doesn't address overdose mortality directly; possible signal-of-permissiveness concerns.

**Option D: Retroactive resentencing of currently-incarcerated**
Existing inmates serving sentences for offenses below the proposed thresholds are automatically resentenced under new guidelines, with parole boards reviewing for early release. Future sentencing rules from Option A or B layer on top. Outcomes: immediate impact on current prison population; aligned with retroactive-equity reasoning; well-documented effect in California Prop 47. Risks: parole-board capacity bottleneck; victims' rights groups oppose; some released inmates re-offend, creating political crisis.

**Option E: Comprehensive harm-reduction package**
Combines: limited decriminalization (Option C), expanded treatment access via tax-funded clinics, supervised consumption sites, naloxone distribution, expanded fentanyl test strips, prescription heroin pilot for chronic users. No retroactive resentencing in initial bill. Outcomes: most ambitious; explicit public-health framing; addresses overdose deaths directly; expensive (~$200-400M/year additional). Risks: politically toughest; conservative jurisdictions and police unions oppose; may need federal waivers; long timeline to outcomes (3-5 years).

## What to provide

Choose one as the proposed framework. Provide 200-400 words of reasoning. Advocate; do not enumerate. Address: why this option, why not the most compelling alternative, what specific outcome 3 years from now would make you regret this choice and what you would argue at that point.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
